what: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
text: flexible Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis (QBA) unmeasured confounding observational studies, described : Hughes RA, Kawabata E, Palmer TM, et al. flexible Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis unmeasured confounding. Statistical Methods Medical Research (review). unmeasured confounding suspected, method produces bias-adjusted estimate exposure effect together interval reflects unmeasured confounding sampling variability. uses informative priors small number bias parameters encode external information unmeasured confounder(s) — , unlike existing software, applies : generalised linear model (glm family) Cox proportional hazards model; binary, continuous, categorical exposure measured confounders; one binary continuous unmeasured confounders, may correlated measured confounders. number bias parameters grow number measured confounders. Status: early development. Monte Carlo QBA implemented; Bayesian QBA paper yet included. companion Stata command name described paper.

title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
text: can install binary version MRC IEU R-universe install source using

title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
text: Monte Carlo replication (paper, section 2.4): draws value every bias parameter prior; simulates proxy unmeasured confounder function exposure ; refits outcome model including simulated proxies, coefficients fixed drawn values (model offset); reads exposure coefficient adds Monte Carlo sampling error. bias-adjusted point estimate median, interval 2.5th 97.5th percentiles, resulting distribution.

title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
text: naive estimate badly biased away true value 0.5; bias-adjusted estimate much closer interval covers truth.
code: library(qbaconfound)  # Simulate data where `u` confounds the x -> y relationship. df <- sim_confounding(n = 1000, beta_x = 0.5, seed = 1)  # The naive model omits the unmeasured confounder u and is biased # (true value is 0.5): coef(lm(y ~ x + c1, df))["x"] #>         x  #> 0.8776387  # Adjust for a single continuous unmeasured confounder, supplying informative # priors for its three bias parameters. fit <- qbaconfound(   y ~ x + c1, data = df, exposure = "x",   confounders = u_continuous(     coef_out = c(0.8, 0.1),   # N(mean, sd) prior for the U -> Y coefficient     coef_exp = c(0.6, 0.05),  # N(mean, sd) prior for the X -> U coefficient     resid_sd = c(0.9, 1.1)    # Uniform(min, max) prior for U's residual SD   ),   reps = 1000, seed = 1 ) fit #> Monte Carlo QBA for unmeasured confounding #> Outcome family: gaussian | replications: 1000 | observations: 1000 #> Unmeasured confounder(s): 1 (continuous) #>  #>  term naive bias-adjusted       95% CI #>     x 0.878         0.401 (0.22, 0.56)

title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
code: qbaconfound(   y ~ x + c1, data = df, exposure = "x", family = binomial(),   confounders = list(     u_continuous(coef_out = c(0.8, 0.1), coef_exp = c(0.6, 0.05),                  resid_sd = c(0.9, 1.1)),     u_binary(coef_out = c(0.4, 0.1), coef_exp = c(0.3, 0.05),              prevalence = c(0.15, 0.25))   ),   reps = 1000, seed = 1 )

title: Monte Carlo Quantitative Bias Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
code: qbaconfound(   survival::Surv(time, status) ~ x + c1, data = surv_df, exposure = "x",   confounders = u_continuous(coef_out = c(0.5, 0.1), coef_exp = c(0.6, 0.05),                              resid_sd = c(0.9, 1.1)),   reps = 1000, seed = 1 )

what: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
text: Conducts flexible Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis (QBA) unmeasured confounding Hughes et al. Given naive analysis model (omits one unmeasured confounders) informative priors small number bias parameters, function returns bias-adjusted estimate exposure effect together interval accounts unmeasured confounding sampling variability.

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
code: qbaconfound(   formula,   data,   exposure = NULL,   confounders,   family = stats::gaussian(),   reps = 1000L,   sampling_error = TRUE,   seed = NULL )

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
text: formula naive analysis model, e.g. y ~ x + c1 + c2 GLM Surv(time, status) ~ x + c1 Cox model. unmeasured confounders included formula. data data frame containing outcome, exposure, measured confounders. exposure Character vector naming exposure term(s) formula whose effect interest. Defaults first term right-hand side. confounders single u_continuous()/u_binary() object, list , describing unmeasured confounder(s). family outcome model family: stats::glm() family object, family name, "cox" Cox proportional hazards model. Ignored (inferred Cox) response survival::Surv() call. reps Number Monte Carlo replications. sampling_error Logical; TRUE (default) Monte Carlo sampling error incorporated step 4 . Set FALSE obtain distribution bias-adjusted point estimates without sampling error. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility.

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
text: object class qbaconfound, list elements including estimates (data frame naive bias-adjusted estimates per exposure term), draws (matrix bias-adjusted estimates across replications), n_failed (number replications whose model fit failed).

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
text: substantive analysis may generalised linear model (stats::glm() family) Cox proportional hazards model. Survival outcomes detected automatically left-hand side formula survival::Surv() call, family can set "cox". Monte Carlo replication (see Hughes et al., section 2.4): draws value every bias parameter prior (priors specified u_continuous() / u_binary()); simulates proxy unmeasured confounder function exposure (continuous proxy normal model, binary proxy Bernoulli model whose intercept reproduces drawn prevalence); refits outcome model including simulated proxies, coefficients fixed drawn values (implemented model offset), reads exposure coefficient standard error; adds Monte Carlo sampling error drawing bias-adjusted estimate normal distribution centred coefficient. point estimate median interval 2.5th 97.5th percentiles resulting distribution bias-adjusted estimates.

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
text: Hughes RA, Kawabata E, Palmer TM, et al. flexible Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis unmeasured confounding. Statistical Methods Medical Research (review).

title: Monte Carlo quantitative bias analysis for unmeasured confounding — qbaconfound
code: df <- sim_confounding(n = 500, beta_x = 0, seed = 1)  # Naive model y ~ x + c1 omits the confounder u; adjust for one continuous U. fit <- qbaconfound(   y ~ x + c1, data = df, exposure = "x",   confounders = u_continuous(coef_out = c(0.8, 0.1),                              coef_exp = c(0.6, 0.05),                              resid_sd = c(0.9, 1.1)),   reps = 200, seed = 1 ) fit #> Monte Carlo QBA for unmeasured confounding #> Outcome family: gaussian | replications: 200 | observations: 500 #> Unmeasured confounder(s): 1 (continuous) #>  #>  term naive bias-adjusted          95% CI #>     x 0.371        -0.099 (-0.305, 0.074)

what: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
title: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
text: small helper simulates dataset one measured confounder c1 one unmeasured confounder u jointly confound exposure-outcome relationship. naive model y ~ x + c1 (omits u) therefore biased exposure effect, making data useful examples tests qbaconfound().

title: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
code: sim_confounding(   n = 1000L,   beta_x = 0,   family = c("gaussian", "binomial"),   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
text: n Number observations. beta_x True exposure effect (linear-predictor scale). family Outcome type: "gaussian" (continuous outcome) "binomial" (binary outcome). seed Optional integer seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
text: data frame columns y, x, c1, u (unmeasured confounder, included can removed mimic unmeasured case). true exposure effect stored attr(, "beta_x").

title: Simulate data with an unmeasured confounder — sim_confounding
code: df <- sim_confounding(n = 1000, beta_x = 0.5, seed = 42) # Naive (biased) versus full (unbiased) model: coef(lm(y ~ x + c1, df))["x"] #>         x  #> 0.8357274  coef(lm(y ~ x + c1 + u, df))["x"] #>        x  #> 0.480904

what: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
title: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
text: Describes prior distributions bias parameters single binary unmeasured confounder, use qbaconfound().

title: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
text: coef_out Length-2 numeric c(mean, sd) giving normal prior coefficient unmeasured confounder outcome model (\(\beta_U\)). Set sd = 0 point-mass (fixed value) prior. coef_exp Normal prior coefficient(s) exposure model unmeasured confounder (\(\alpha_X\)). single exposure term, length-2 numeric c(mean, sd). categorical exposure several terms, matrix one row c(mean, sd) per exposure term, list length-2 vectors. prevalence Length-2 numeric giving prior marginal prevalence \(\pi\) binary unmeasured confounder. prev_dist = "uniform" (default) c(min, max) uniform prior; prev_dist = "beta" c(, b) beta prior. prev_dist Prior family prevalence: "uniform" "beta".

title: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
text: object class qba_u describing binary unmeasured confounder.

title: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
text: u_continuous() coefficient U outcome model (coef_out) coefficient(s) exposure model U (coef_exp) bias parameters. binary confounder third bias parameter marginal prevalence U (prevalence, .e. \(\pi\)) rather residual standard deviation. prevalence usually easier elicit readily reported literature intercept logistic model; intercept needed reproduce drawn prevalence derived internally.

title: Specify a binary unmeasured confounder — u_binary
code: u_binary(coef_out = c(0.7, 0.1), coef_exp = c(0.4, 0.05),          prevalence = c(0.15, 0.25)) #> <binary unmeasured confounder> #>   coef_out (beta_U): N(mean = 0.7, sd = 0.1) #>   coef_exp (alpha_X)[1]: N(mean = 0.4, sd = 0.05) #>   prevalence (pi): Uniform(0.15, 0.25)

what: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
title: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
text: Describes prior distributions bias parameters single continuous unmeasured confounder, use qbaconfound().

title: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
text: coef_out Length-2 numeric c(mean, sd) giving normal prior coefficient unmeasured confounder outcome model (\(\beta_U\)). Set sd = 0 point-mass (fixed value) prior. coef_exp Normal prior coefficient(s) exposure model unmeasured confounder (\(\alpha_X\)). single exposure term, length-2 numeric c(mean, sd). categorical exposure several terms, matrix one row c(mean, sd) per exposure term, list length-2 vectors. resid_sd Length-2 numeric giving prior residual standard deviation \(\eta\). resid_dist = "uniform" (default) c(min, max) uniform prior \(\eta\). resid_dist = "gamma" c(shape, scale) gamma prior residual precision \(1/\eta^2\). resid_dist Prior family residual standard deviation: either "uniform" (\(\eta\)) "gamma" (precision \(1/\eta^2\)).

title: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
text: object class qba_u describing continuous unmeasured confounder.

title: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
text: bias model relates unmeasured confounder U study data three bias parameters: coefficient U outcome model (coef_out, .e. \(\beta_U\)), coefficient(s) exposure model U (coef_exp, .e. \(\alpha_X\)), residual standard deviation U given exposure (resid_sd, .e. \(\eta\)). Values parameters estimated data drawn prior distributions specified .

title: Specify a continuous unmeasured confounder — u_continuous
code: u_continuous(coef_out = c(0.8, 0.1), coef_exp = c(0.3, 0.05),              resid_sd = c(0.9, 1.1)) #> <continuous unmeasured confounder> #>   coef_out (beta_U): N(mean = 0.8, sd = 0.1) #>   coef_exp (alpha_X)[1]: N(mean = 0.3, sd = 0.05) #>   resid_sd (eta): Uniform(0.9, 1.1)
